speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November the 26th, 1996. The liberator is Morris Leslie Primack, interviewer Carol Hurst, in Bushy Heath, Hertfordshire, England. And the language is English. My name is Carol Hurst. Today is November the 26th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Morris Leslie Primack in Bushy Heath, Hertfordshire, England. And the interview is being conducted in English. Can you tell me your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Morris, known as Leslie Primack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Morris, M-O-double R-I-S L-E-S-L-I-E P-R-I-M-A-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 16th of June, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And the city and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Riegate in the county of Surrey, which is in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you spell Riegate in Surrey?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-I-E-G-A-T-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had two older sisters and a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister is Zena. The next one is Rosie. And my younger brother is Sidney.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And how did your parents support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a working tailor in the early days. And that's as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked as the-- naturally, as the mother of the house, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. She was a housewife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, we had a small grocery store for about four or five years. But then that closed. And my father was then the supporter of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you tell me, how did your family identify as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Orthodox. My, my paternal grandfather was a rabbi. But that as an honorary title. He wasn't, he wasn't ordained. But he was a very learned man. And when he came to England from Poland, he set up a small Talmud Torah in Bethnal Green, which is in East London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what a Talmud Torah was for the tape, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's Hebrew classes, yes, to teach Jewish language and the religion to young Jewish boys in preparation for their bar mitzvah when they became the age of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the kind of school that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very elementary-- had about four-- 40 or 45 boys in the class. As far as I remember, our education then at the time was fairly basic, you know-- English and math and a bit of geography. Apart from that, I can't remember very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did you go to Hebrew classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I can remember, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about outside of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, no. No, I don't recollect any anti-Semitism until I was well into my-- 17-- when I was about 17 or 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Blackshirts [INAUDIBLE] in East London. And that's when I joined the [INAUDIBLE] Zionist organization to compete with that. And that was about the time when Mosley was the head of the Blackshirt movement in the, in the, in London, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me any more about your own personal experience at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was involved with other young men and women of our ages. And we, we tried to combat what was going on there. We appeared at rallies and the demonstrations to show support for the Jewish people, mainly who lived in the East End of London. And that's where Mosley was operating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other non-Jews active in fighting against Mosley?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure they were. Yes, I'm quite sure they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What hobbies did you enjoy as a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports, football-- I was very keen on cycling. And I was also very keen on the Boy Scouts Association. We did outdoor events and went camping, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your aspirations after leaving school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I had very much. I know that the economic times were very difficult in the, in the early '30s. And the idea was just to go out and work and earn some money. I wasn't a very bright scholar, so there was no further education as far as I was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what occupation did you take up after leaving school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, most boys [? around that time, ?] we went into the clothing industry, most of it anyway. And I-- my father had a small tailoring unit. And I went to work for him. And I did other jobs. I found I didn't like the tailoring business, although ultimately, I did finish up doing that. I found odd jobs in different factories doing different jobs, nothing of any particular interest. When I was about 15, my father thought that I should have some technical knowledge of the clothing industry. And he sent me to a school which specialized in that. It also included techniques of management. And when I was 18, I had the all-important task of being appointed assistant factory manager in the clothing industry, which is where I met my future wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? What events [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I worked in that company for-- until I was 21. There was a great deal of unrest in Europe. It was very evident that war was coming. Not exactly when, but it was coming. And the government decreed that all men of the age of 21 would join the army for six-months training, obviously, in preparation of war. And as I was 21 at the time, on the 1st of July, 1939, which prior to the outbreak of war, I, I was conscripted into the army. I joined the Royal Corps of Signals in Catterick in Yorkshire. And I trained as a dispatch rider. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our training continued. And then September the third of that year, war was declared. On November the 5th, I was with a unit. And we embarked for France as the initial unit of the British Expeditionary Force. And we landed in Cherbourg. We traveled across France. And we established our headquarters in Arras, which is a  town in Northern France. And my, my unit was not a combat one. We were communications. We provided the support and communications from headquarters to the forward troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the winter of '39 was comparatively quiet. The German army was busy elsewhere, mainly in Poland. We had a very bad winter, '39, '40. And then of course, in the spring of 1940, the German army broke through the Maginot Line, which is in Southern France. And their rapid-- advance was quite rapid. They, they were a superior force. And they swept across Southern France, up towards Paris and towards the, and towards the coast. The British troops were outnumbered. And we moved back all the time until we reached the coast. And we set our defenses along the, the Dunkirk, French-Belgium border. As I say, my unit was one of communications. And we set up a radio station in an old building just back from the beach. We were in contact with the war office in London, who transferred instructions to us. And we would have to go out and find the units. We-- I was still a dispatch rider at the time. And we would have to go out and find the units to convey messages and dispatches. And in many cases, when we arrived at the destination, they were no longer there. The German troops had arrived. And I beat a hasty retreat. And eventually, through the month of May, the troops were falling back to the beaches. The government had organized ships, the navy merchant ship to come across the channel to lift off as many servicemen as possible. The conditions on the beaches were quite horrific. We were without aerial cover. The, the German Air Force was quite superior in numbers. We were bombed. We were machine gunned. Casualties were extremely high. The big boats would come in and take up as many men as possible. I recall that Dunkirk was on fire. The oil refineries were ablaze. The city was on fire-- huge clouds of smoke across the area. My unit stayed behind because we were still on active service. But many of the forward troops were getting away. They were either swimming out to the boats, many of them machine gunned by the German Air Force. Many of the boats were bombed and were sunk. And the situation there was quite horrific, you know. Bodies were floating in on the tide. And the beaches were covered with wounded and the dead. And on June the first, we were told to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] destroy our equipment, to destroy our units and our bikes. And we should walk towards Dunkirk. At that time, we were just on the French-Belgium border in a town called Lapan. And on the morning of June the 1st, we began walking. It was seven miles where we were. And we got to the port some time in the afternoon. And there was just one boat. It was an old paddle steamer, you know, with the paddles rotating at the side. And we managed to get on board. Again, we were bombed and machine gunned. Eventually, the boat managed to get out of the dock and made its way across the channel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, we were bombed and machine gunned. A couple of Spitfires came out. And I remember them. They escorted us back. And eventually, we arrived at Folkeston. We were glad to be home. And I survived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt your life was under threat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: For all that time. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the unit was reformed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next thing I did, I got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- my wife and I, we were married on the 21st of June, 1940, at the Devonshire Road Synagogue in East London. The service took place during an air raid. And the reception that followed was adequate for the family. Food was rationed, so we did as best we could. And my parents did as best they could. And after two days leave, I went back to my unit. And from then onwards, I was part of the defenses of the UK. And we were operational. And for most of that time, I worked with the Royal Corps of Signals in the war office, maintaining communications with the troops throughout the, throughout the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife doing during the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did part-time work as a nurse in the Jewish hospital in Stepney Green in East London, in addition to doing her regular work-- again, was in the clothing industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep in contact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately, yes-- because being working with the war office signals, I was billeted in London. So I was able to get home fairly frequently.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you billeted in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: For about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you go back to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to-- we were preparation for the Normandy invasion. And again, as I said before, I was not a combat, more one of communications. And I didn't go over to Normandy until perhaps sometime in middle of July, by which time, some of the most fiercest of fighting had already taken place. And we were the aftermath. And we followed the forward troops as they traveled across France and up towards-- eventually, into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the places you traveled through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] across France, yes, there's many town. There was Amiens, There was Abbeville. There was [INAUDIBLE]. There was Lille. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually, we moved up into Belgium. And we were billeted or stationed just outside Brussels. I don't remember the actual name, but we were there mainly because the 21st Army Group was-- the headquarters were in Brussels. And we were their support team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, that was 1944, at the autumn of 1944, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware at any time of what was happening to the Jews in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of stories was coming out. Nothing was really corroborated-- lots and lots of stories. Of course, we knew about the Kindertransport. We knew about the children being brought out of Germany. In fact, my parents had a young girl from Germany. And they took her in and looked after her. So we were aware of that all the time, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any atrocities against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What rank were you in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the army I was a signalman, which is equal to private.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews in your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned as to your, your safety, particularly because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really-- I was aware of it. I was the only Jew, which is why-- there was anti-Semitism in my unit, yes, which is why I didn't take promotion. It was offered to me, but I felt vulnerable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Perhaps I was wrong in hindsight. Perhaps I should have taken promotion and asserted my position. But perhaps I was young at the time and didn't look at it from that point of view.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned for your safety had you been taken prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you are a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1939, before I went out to France, I was asked whether I-- you know, we had dog tags, identification tags. And of course, the word Jew was on it. And I was asked whether I would like it to be changed. And I said yes. And it was changed to RC, Roman Catholic, yes. And my AB64, which is a soldier's pay book, had my parents' birthplace as Russia. And that was changed as well. And I found that the, the authorities were extremely helpful and thoughtful at that time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after that, after your service in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked with headquarters in Brussels. And in the spring of '45, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had, of course, a Jewish service in the Bon Marche. There was a department store in Brussels owned by some Jewish people. Their name was Vaxelaire. And at that time, there was a Jewish forces club in Brussels. The organizer, the manager, was a man called Sam [? Ansel-- ?] great fellow. He organized a Seder service for about 500 Jewish servicemen and women who came from all parts of France, Belgium. And that was a great get-together. And shortly after that, 12 men in my squad were instructed to report to the medical room. We were to have inoculations. We weren't told why. They just said, well, you know, you're going to need this in the near future. And the following day, the 12 of us, we loaded six trucks with radio equipment. And we were told we were going up to a place called Bergen-Belsen in Germany. And at that time, the name didn't mean anything to us. The journey took about six hours. And as we neared our destination, we noticed a very strange smell in the atmosphere. We just couldn't decide what it was. It wasn't a gas because we-- we-- it definitely wasn't a wartime gas. It was something much stronger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We later found out it was chlorine. And as we approached the-- we didn't know what to expect. We got to-- we saw signs on the road which said, forbidden area, military area, keep out, typhus. And then we realized we were getting into something quite, quite serious. The military police directed us to the, a parking place. And we were told to report to the camp commander. It was a barbed wire area. And the gates were open. There were watchtowers. They were unmanned. And we walked through the gates. And the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sight was quite devastating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] they were walking about in these striped uniform, those who could walk. They were walking skeletons, dreadful conditions. There were people laying around on the ground in grotesque situations, grotesque positions. And it was, it was totally unexpected. We hadn't prepared for this sort of thing. And we just stood there, unbelievable. It just, it just didn't, just didn't seem possible. The smell was quite strong. But by this time, we'd become used to it. And way over on the left, there were a whole line of barracks, wooden dark-brown barracks. And there was smoke. Fires were burning in the area, a lot of troops moving around. We saw German soldiers picking up the dead in-- on trolleys and taking them over to the right-hand side of the camp. We saw bulldozers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pushing the bodies into a huge pit. The Red Cross workers, they were first aid workers. The place was buzzing with activity. And in the middle of all this, there were all these poor people sitting, staring, not really knowing what was going on. Some of them were a little more alert than others. And they came and tried to speak to us. But we didn't have the language. But they shook our hands. And we tried to-- we were, we were helpless.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were then instructed to take our radio equipment to a, a building. And we said, well, why are we here? Can we help? And they said, no, you-- there were certain areas that you must not go to. It's absolutely forbidden. We were given menial tasks around the place. We were then told to go back to our trucks and stay there until we were given further instructions.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed out there for several hours. And then we were told to drive to a different part of the camp where some of the prisoners were in a better condition than those that we had seen previously. And we later found out that some of the prisoners had, had only recently arrived. And some of them wore decent clothing and looked in a much better condition than the others that we had seen. And we spent that night living in our trucks, awaiting instructions. Then, the following day we were told that we would be taking some of the prisoners back to Brussels. We tried to make our trucks, which were not designed to carry passengers-- we tried to make it as comfortable as we could for them. We got hold of some sacking and some boxes. And we made some kind of seating arrangement. And then we-- the, these prisoners were brought forward. We were each given papers of the 20 or so women. They were only-- they were all women, young women and older women. And there were 20 women on my truck. And we were given the papers of each one of them. And as I was shuffling through the papers, I noticed one of the names of the women there was named Sophie, which was my mother's name. And I sort of attached myself to this young woman. And I told her I'd look after her. But didn't mean to say I didn't look after all the others, but this one in particular. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had rations for our journey back to-- we took water aboard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the journey back, we were, we were told that we were to drive into Brussels and go straight to the [INAUDIBLE], which is the Grand Place, which is just a square outside the north station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was unable to converse with this young woman, because we had a language problem. My Yiddish wasn't all that wonderful. But somehow or other, I got to know that she was Polish and that her family had come from Krakow. And she said she have no family. On the journey, we had no idea what were going to happen to these, these people. But as we got nearer to Brussels, to the center, we began to realize what was happening. As the trucks drove onto the square, there were hundreds of civilian people waiting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As the trucks came to a halt and we dropped back, these civilians just came forward and just took the first person that-- just stood next to them. But I kept Sophie to one side. And later, I was able to get her into the home of some Jewish people that I had met while I was stationed in Brussels. And she was a very poor, little thing, very tired and very frightened. And these people, whose name I forget, made her comfortable. And they were able to converse with her. And two days later when I went back to the home, the, the story began to emerge, that indeed, she did come from Krakow. Her parents and-- I think it was two brothers and a sister, they were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did she know what their fate was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she had no idea. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although she only looked about 15 or 16, she was much older. She-- I think she was about 25 to 30. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when it came to relatives, the only relative that she was aware of was an uncle who had emigrated to Australia in '37 and '38 and had repeatedly written to them to encourage them to come out. He sent them money. But they kept on postponing the situation, thinking things would get better. And they didn't. And the family was split. And she was taken away. And she had-- she didn't know what her uncle's name was or where he was living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I decided to do something about it. There was no civilian mail at that time. So through the Army Post Office, I wrote to the chairman or president of the Zionist Federation of Australia and gave him the story and asked him what he could do to locate this man. We had no name, no first name, nothing-- didn't even know the name of the-- oh, yes. We did know the names of the-- Sophie's parents, yeah. And he wrote back that-- this, this whole thing took quite several months, during which time Sophie was looked after by these friends of mine. And eventually, I had a letter back from the-- from Australia to say that the only thing they could do there would be to advertise in the with whatever information was available and see what came out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we stop there for the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
